101: King of the Huns, known as 'the Scourge of God,' whose empire stretched from Central Europe to the Black Sea.
102: Byzantine emperor who compiled the Justinian Code, the foundation of modern civil law, and built the Hagia Sophia.
103: Andalusian philosopher and physician whose commentaries on Aristotle profoundly influenced medieval European thought.
104: Sultan of Egypt and Syria who recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders, renowned for his nobility and chivalry.
105: Moroccan explorer and geographer who traveled over 120,000 km across Africa, Asia, and Europe, leaving an invaluable record of his journeys.
106: English writer, author of The Canterbury Tales, considered the father of English literature.
107: Florentine statesman and patron of the arts under whose sponsorship Botticelli, Michelangelo, and Leonardo da Vinci flourished.
108: Spanish conquistador who brought about the fall of the Aztec Empire and the incorporation of Mexico into the Spanish Crown.
109: Spanish conquistador of the Inca Empire who founded Lima and completed the conquest of South America for Spain.
110: Holy Roman Emperor and King of Spain who ruled the most extensive empire in Europe of his time.
111: Queen of England during the Elizabethan Golden Age who defeated the Spanish Armada and was a patron of Shakespeare.
112: King of Spain, Portugal, and Naples who ruled the empire on which the sun never set and built El Escorial.
113: Spanish Golden Age painter whose work Las Meninas is considered one of the most important paintings in history.
114: Tsar of Russia who modernized the country, founded Saint Petersburg, and transformed Russia into a European power.
115: Empress of Russia who led the country to its golden age, expanded the empire, and patronized the arts and education.
116: First President of the United States and commander of the Continental Army during the American Revolution.
117: Third President of the United States and principal author of the Declaration of Independence.
118: Leader of Spanish-American independence and liberator of Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia.
119: General and liberator who led the independence movements of Argentina, Chile, and Peru during the wars of emancipation.
120: First Lady of Argentina and activist for workers' and women's rights, a cultural icon of Latin America.
121: Catalan architect and foremost exponent of Modernisme who designed the Sagrada Família and Park Güell.
122: Dutch Post-Impressionist painter, one of the most influential figures in Western art, who painted The Starry Night.
123: French painter and founder of Impressionism, renowned for his series of water lilies and cathedral paintings.
124: French sculptor considered one of the fathers of modern sculpture and author of The Thinker.
125: German philosopher whose ideas on the Übermensch, the death of God, and morality transformed Western thought.
126: Danish philosopher considered the father of existentialism and a critic of institutional religion and Hegelian philosophy.
127: American inventor who developed the incandescent light bulb, the phonograph, and the modern electrical system.
128: Scottish-American scientist and inventor credited with inventing the telephone and founder of the Bell Telephone Company.
129: Wilbur and Orville Wright, aviation pioneers who accomplished the first controlled powered flight in 1903.
130: American industrialist who revolutionized manufacturing with the assembly line and the Ford Model T.
131: Mexican painter famous for her self-portraits laden with symbolism and personal pain.
132: Colombian writer and 1982 Nobel Prize in Literature laureate, master of magical realism and author of One Hundred Years of Solitude.
133: Argentine writer and one of the most influential authors of the 20th century, master of the short story and the essay.
134: American animator, producer, and entrepreneur, pioneer of animation and founder of the Disney empire.
135: British actor, director, and composer, one of the most recognizable icons of silent cinema with his character the Tramp.
136: American film director and master of cinema who directed 2001: A Space Odyssey and A Clockwork Orange.
137: British musician, singer, and songwriter, co-founder of The Beatles, one of the most influential bands in history.
138: British singer and songwriter, lead vocalist of Queen, and one of the most legendary voices in rock.
139: Entrepreneur and co-founder of Apple who revolutionized personal computing, digital music, and mobile telephony.
140: British theoretical physicist who made fundamental contributions to the understanding of black holes and cosmology.
141: Princess of Wales, a global icon for her humanitarian work and charisma whose death shocked the world.
142: American astronomer and science communicator, author of Cosmos, who popularized science for the general public.
143: Italian physician and educator who created the Montessori method, revolutionizing early childhood education worldwide.
144: British nurse and founder of modern nursing who reformed healthcare practices during the Crimean War.
145: British mathematician considered the first computer programmer for her notes on Babbage's analytical engine.
146: British mathematician and inventor who designed the analytical engine, considered a forerunner of the modern computer.
147: Chinese politician, military leader, and revolutionary, leader of the Communist Revolution and founder of the People's Republic of China.
148: Pharaoh of Egypt whose intact tomb, discovered in 1922, revealed the most spectacular treasures of ancient Egypt.
149: Queen of ancient Egypt and wife of Pharaoh Akhenaten, whose bust is one of the most recognizable icons of Egyptian art.
150: Roman gladiator who led the largest slave rebellion against the Roman Republic, known as the Third Servile War.
151: Greek philosopher and mathematician, considered one of the Seven Sages of Greece and the first known Western philosopher.
152: Athenian statesman, lawgiver, and poet who laid the foundations of Athenian democracy through his reforms.
153: Greek lyric poet from Lesbos, one of the most celebrated voices of ancient poetry, called the 'Tenth Muse' by Plato.
154: Pre-Socratic philosopher and pupil of Thales, a pioneer of cosmology and cartography.
155: Greek fabulist whose celebrated animal fables have inspired world literature.
156: Pre-Socratic philosopher known for the saying 'everything flows,' who argued that change is the essence of the universe.
157: Athenian statesman whose rule marked the Golden Age of Athens; he built the Parthenon and consolidated democracy.
158: Greek playwright considered the father of tragedy and author of the Oresteia.
159: Greek playwright and author of Oedipus Rex and Antigone, one of the three great tragedians of Athenian theatre.
160: Greek playwright and author of Medea and The Trojan Women who innovated tragedy with psychologically complex characters.
161: Greek comic playwright, foremost representative of Old Comedy, and author of The Clouds and Lysistrata.
162: Pre-Socratic philosopher who first proposed the atomic theory of matter, millennia before its confirmation.
163: Greek biographer whose work Lives of the Eminent Philosophers is the principal source on ancient philosophers.
164: Roman general who defeated Hannibal at the Battle of Zama, ending the Second Punic War.
165: Roman politician and writer, an implacable defender of republican traditions and of the destruction of Carthage.
166: Roman general and statesman, member of the First Triumvirate alongside Caesar and Crassus, and later Caesar's rival.
167: Roman general, ally of Caesar, and lover of Cleopatra whose defeat at Actium by Octavian changed the history of Rome.
168: Roman senator, conspirator, and one of the assassins of Julius Caesar on the Ides of March, 44 BC.
169: Stoic philosopher, writer, and Roman politician who was tutor to Emperor Nero; his Letters to Lucilius are a philosophical gem.
170: Roman emperor, symbol of imperial despotism and madness, assassinated by the Praetorian Guard after four years of rule.
171: Roman emperor famous for the Great Fire of Rome and the persecution of Christians, who ultimately died by suicide.
172: Roman emperor and founder of the Flavian dynasty who initiated construction of the Colosseum.
173: Roman emperor under whose rule the Roman Empire reached its greatest territorial extent.
174: Roman emperor who built Hadrian's Wall in Britain and consolidated the frontiers of the Empire.
175: Roman historian whose Annals and Histories are essential sources for understanding the Roman Empire.
176: Roman naturalist and military officer, author of Natural History, who died investigating the eruption of Vesuvius.
177: Stoic philosopher born into slavery whose teachings, recorded by his disciple Arrian, remain influential today.
178: Anglo-Saxon monk and scholar, father of English history, whose Ecclesiastical History is a cornerstone of medieval literature.
179: Anglo-Saxon scholar and theologian, adviser to Charlemagne and promoter of the Carolingian Renaissance.
180: Castilian knight and legendary military leader of the Reconquista whose deeds inspired the epic poem El Cantar de Mio Cid.
181: Queen of France and England, one of the most powerful women of the Middle Ages and mother of Richard the Lionheart.
182: King of England, leader of the Third Crusade, and legendary knight who died from an arrow wound during a siege.
183: Holy Roman Emperor called 'stupor mundi' whose Sicilian court was a beacon of medieval culture.
184: Italian friar and founder of the Franciscan Order, a universal symbol of voluntary poverty and love of nature.
185: Franciscan philosopher and scientist who championed the experimental method as a forerunner of modern science.
186: Franciscan philosopher famous for 'Ockham's razor,' the principle of logical simplicity.
187: Italian poet and humanist, father of Renaissance humanism, whose sonnets to Laura are universal classics.
188: Italian writer and author of the Decameron, one of the great narrative works of the Trecento.
189: Florentine architect and engineer who designed the dome of Florence Cathedral, a technical marvel of the Renaissance.
190: Florentine sculptor, master of the early Renaissance, and pioneer of bronze casting and sculptural realism.
191: Florentine painter and author of The Birth of Venus and Primavera, landmark works of the Renaissance.
192: Venetian painter, master of colour, and key figure of the Venetian Renaissance who portrayed European royalty.
193: Venetian Mannerist painter known for the dramatic energy and light effects in his works.
194: Greek-Spanish painter whose unique style, featuring elongated figures and intense colours, anticipated Expressionism.
195: Dutch humanist whose Praise of Folly is a classic; he advocated reform of the Church without breaking with Rome.
196: English humanist and statesman, author of Utopia, who was executed for opposing Henry VIII.
197: Portuguese navigator and the first European to reach India by sea, inaugurating the Portuguese colonial era.
198: Italian explorer whose voyages demonstrated that the lands discovered by Columbus were a new continent; America bears his name.
199: German astronomer and mathematician who formulated the three laws of planetary motion.
200: Danish astronomer who made the most precise pre-telescopic observations and was Kepler's mentor.
